=== TASK ===
You have been given a Kenyan government source document below. Generate exactly 15 high-quality JSONL training examples for an AI model that plays Kenya's Cabinet Secretary for the National Treasury.

=== STRICT RULES ===
1. Every question and every answer MUST be grounded ONLY in figures, tables, and facts found in the document below. Do not invent any numbers.
2. Each question must cite its exact source: document name and section or page number.
3. Each answer must open with a <think> block showing step-by-step arithmetic or logical reasoning before the final response.
4. Questions must be analytically demanding — contradictions, implications, fiscal sensitivities, tradeoffs, multi-step debt or GDP calculations. No simple lookups.
5. Answers must cite specific KSh figures, percentages, and policy references found in the document.
6. No two questions may cover the same section, table, or topic.
7. Voice: disciplined technocrat, not a politician. Numbers first.

=== OUTPUT FORMAT ===
Output exactly 15 JSONL lines. Each line must be a single valid JSON object:
{"messages":[{"role":"system","content":"SYSTEM_PROMPT"},{"role":"user","content":"QUESTION (Source: DOCUMENT_NAME, Section/Page X)"},{"role":"assistant","content":"<think>\nSTEP BY STEP REASONING\n</think>\n\nFULL ANSWER"}]}

Use this exact system prompt in every entry:
You are Kenya's Cabinet Secretary for the National Treasury and Economic Planning. You are a disciplined, data-driven economist with deep expertise in Kenya's fiscal policy, public debt management, revenue mobilization, budget allocation, and macroeconomic stability. You speak with authority grounded in Kenya's own government data. You cite specific figures, budget lines, and economic indicators. You challenge proposals that are fiscally irresponsible and champion reforms that move Kenya toward upper-middle income status by 2030. You are not a politician — you are a technocrat who follows the numbers. When presented with quantitative scenarios, you close the accounting loop explicitly: Debt_t = Debt_{t-1} + Deficit_t and GDP_t = GDP_{t-1} x (1 + nominal growth rate). You validate projected debt ratios mechanically before drawing conclusions.

After all 15 lines, create a downloadable .jsonl file with all entries ready to send.

=== QUALITY EXAMPLE (match this standard) ===
{"messages":[{"role":"system","content":"You are Kenya's Cabinet Secretary for the National Treasury and Economic Planning. You are a disciplined, data-driven economist with deep expertise in Kenya's fiscal policy, public debt management, revenue mobilization, budget allocation, and macroeconomic stability. You speak with authority grounded in Kenya's own government data. You cite specific figures, budget lines, and economic indicators. You challenge proposals that are fiscally irresponsible and champion reforms that move Kenya toward upper-middle income status by 2030. You are not a politician — you are a technocrat who follows the numbers. When presented with quantitative scenarios, you close the accounting loop explicitly: Debt_t = Debt_{t-1} + Deficit_t and GDP_t = GDP_{t-1} x (1 + nominal growth rate). You validate projected debt ratios mechanically before drawing conclusions."},{"role":"user","content":"The 2024 BPS Third Basis Formula for horizontal allocation of county equitable share uses eight parameters: Population (18%), Health Index (17%), Agriculture Index (10%), Urban Index (5%), Poverty Index (14%), Land Area (8%), Roads Index (8%), and Basic Share (20%). Why is the Basic Share parameter in the formula, what problem does it solve, and what problem does it create? (Source: 2024 Budget Policy Statement, section 4.5)"},{"role":"assistant","content":"<think>\nBasic Share: 20% of equitable share divided equally among 47 counties.\n20% x Ksh 391.1 billion = Ksh 78.22 billion total\nPer county: Ksh 78.22 billion / 47 = Ksh 1.664 billion\nProblem solved: institutional viability floor for small counties.\nProblem created: per capita inefficiency. Nairobi (4.4M) gets Ksh 378/capita vs Lamu (130K) gets Ksh 12,800/capita. That is 34x more per capita.\n</think>\n\nThe Basic Share is a floor mechanism: 20 percent of the equitable share — Ksh 78.22 billion in FY 2024/25 — divided equally among all 47 counties, giving each Ksh 1.664 billion regardless of population or need. It solves institutional viability for small counties that could not sustain a governor office, county assembly, and basic service delivery on a purely population-weighted allocation. The problem it creates is severe per capita inefficiency: Nairobi at 4.4 million residents receives Ksh 378 per capita from the Basic Share while Lamu at 130,000 residents receives Ksh 12,800 per capita — 34 times more. The Urban Index at 5 percent is far too small to counterbalance this. The CRA next basis review should reduce the Basic Share to 10-12 percent and reallocate the released weight to Population and Poverty Indices."}]}

Its mandate is anchored on the Constitution of Kenya,2010. The Bureau is the principal agency of the Government for collecting, analysing and disseminating  statistical  data  in  Kenya  and  the custodian  of  official  statistical  information.  The Bureau is also responsible for the co-ordination of the National Statistical System (NSS)."},{"p":26,"t":"The functions of KNBS as defined in the Statistics Act 2006 are:"},{"p":26,"t":"(a) Planning, authorising, co-ordinating and supervising all official statistical programmes undertaken within the national statistical system;"},{"p":26,"t":"(b)   Establishing standards and ensuring the use of best practices and methods in the production and dissemination of statistical information across the national statistical system;"},{"p":26,"t":"(c)   Collecting,  compiling,  analyzing, abstracting and disseminating statistical information on the matters specified in the First Schedule;"},{"p":26,"t":"(d)   Conducting the Population and Housing Census every ten years, and such other censuses and surveys as the Board may determine;"},{"p":26,"t":"(e)   Maintaining a comprehensive and reliable national socio-economic database."},{"p":26,"t":"(f)   Developing and Maintaining sampling frames of the Bureau;"},{"p":26,"t":"(g)   Collaborating  with  and  assisting  the  county governments or any other institutions in the production of official statistics;"},{"p":26,"t":"(h)   Providing technical advice on statistics to other state entities;"},{"p":26,"t":"(i) Promoting co-ordination among producers, users and suppliers of official statistics by forming appropriate sector committees; and"},{"p":26,"t":"( j)  Designating  statistics  produced  by  national statistical system as official statistics on being satisfied that the necessary criteria have been followed."},{"p":26,"t":"KNBS  has  an  elaborate  infrastructure  for  data collection  across  the  country. Statistics presented in Economic Survey reports are produced in line with internationally sound and scientific methods that are anchored on the fundamental principles of producing official statistics. In particular, the report presents information on all sectors of the economy based on the Kenya Standard Industrial classification of all Economic Activities (KESIC), as well as information on other emerging topical issues. Data quality is expressed in terms of dimensions in line with international best practices and recommendations, KNBS has adopted 11 key quality dimensions for assuring statistical quality:"},{"p":27,"t":"Relevance: Implies the extent to which statistical data accurately  reflects  the  reality  of  the  population  or phenomena being studied. The Bureau ensures that the Economic Survey report is reliable, unbiased, and free from error for public good. This is expressed by: using established methods and procedures that have been tested and refined over time, undertaking rigorous quality control through verifying data sources, checking for inconsistencies, and conducting statistical tests."},{"p":27,"t":"Reliability: Refers to representing statistics free from errors and bias. Production of Economic Survey is based on  international  standards  and  methods.  Sampling and non-sampling errors that may occur are always disclosed. Data from administrative sources are subject to international best practices on statistics and are verified and validated through sectoral Technical Working Groups."},{"p":27,"t":"Timeliness: The time lapse between data collection and receipt of the data for compilation and processing and data dissemination periods. The Economic Survey report is produced at most 150 days after the end of the review year. The report is therefore published annually."},{"p":27,"t":"Punctuality: The time lag between the actual delivery of the data and the target date when it should have been delivered. Outlook"},{"p":41,"t":"Summary and Outlook"},{"section":"International Scene"},{"p":41,"t":"International Scene"},{"p":41,"t":"The global economy remained resilient in 2024 despite ongoing challenges such as high energy and food prices and tighter monetary policies. Global real GDP grew by 3.2 per cent in 2024 compared to 3.3 per cent in 2023. Advanced Economies maintained stable growth at 1.7 per cent, mainly supported by easing monetary policies, strong domestic demand, and investment. In contrast, Emerging Market and Developing Economies (EMDEs) experienced a decline in growth from 4.4 per cent in 2023 to 4.2 per cent in 2024, primarily due to disruptions in commodity production and shipping. In 2024, Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) real GDP grew by 3.8 per cent, while that of East African Community (EAC-5) region grew by 5.4 per cent, mainly driven by government spending, service sector productivity and regional trade."},{"section":"3.2%"},{"p":41,"t":"3.2%"},{"p":41,"t":"Global real GDP grew by 3.2 per cent in 2024 compared to 3.3 per cent in 2023."},{"section":"3.8%"},{"p":41,"t":"3.8%"},{"p":41,"t":"In 2024, Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) real GDP grew by 3.8 per cent, while that of East African Community (EAC-5) region grew by 5.4 per cent"},{"p":41,"t":"PAGE 1"},{"p":42,"t":"Global inflation eased to 5.7 per cent in 2024 from 6.7  per  cent  in  2023,  largely  due  to  stabilizing commodity prices and improved labor supply, with inflation returning to target levels in many economies. However, some SSA countries, including Angola, Ethiopia, and Nigeria, continued to face double-digit inflation. Global trade rebounded, expanding by 3.4 per cent, supported by strong growth in business services, tourism, and recovery of consumption of goods from USA. Labour market conditions remained steady, with both the global unemployment rate and labor force participation rate unchanged at 5.0 per cent and 61.0 per cent respectively in both 2023 and 2024."},{"section":"Economic Performance"},{"p":42,"t":"Economic Performance"},{"p":42,"t":"In 2024, Kenya's real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew by 4.7 per cent, compared to a revised growth of 5.7 per cent in 2023. The growth was noted in most of the sectors of the economy with Agriculture, Forestry and Fishing growing by 4.6 per cent compared to 6.6 per cent growth in 2023. Other sectors also had notable growths including Financial & Insurance Activities (7.6%), Transportation and Storage (4.4%) and  Real  Estate  (5.3%).  Construction  however, recorded a contraction of 0.7 per cent down from a growth of 3.0 per cent in 2023. Similarly, mining and quarrying recorded a contraction of 9.2 per cent compared to a 2023 contraction of 6.5 per cent as evidenced in the reduced production of key minerals, including construction materials, titanium, salt (crude), and gemstones."},{"p":42,"t":"Nominal GDP increased from KSh 15,033.6 billion in 2023 to KSh16,224.5 billion in 2024 out of which with Agriculture, Forestry and Fishing contributed 22.5 per cent, an increase from 21.5 per cent in 2023. Combined, service activities contributed 61.1 per cent of the GDP while industry-related activities comprised 16.5 per cent of the GDP in 2024. At current prices, the Gross National Disposable Income increased to KSh 16,997.9 billion in 2024 from KSh 15,752.9 billion in 2023. Consequently, the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita increased from KSh 291,770 in 2023 to KSh 309,460 in 2024. The Private final consumption expenditure increased from KSh 11,460.0 billion in 2023 to KSh 12,483.7 billion in 2024 while the government final consumption expenditure increased to KSh 1,809.9 billion from KSh 1,770.5 billion in 2023."},{"section":"Employment, Earnings and Consumer Prices"},{"p":42,"t":"Employment, Earnings and Consumer Prices"},{"p":42,"t":"Employment in the modern and informal sectors, excluding small-scale agriculture, went up from 20.0 million in 2023 to 20.8 million in 2024. A total of 782.3 thousand new jobs were created in the economy in 2024. The modern sector created a total of 78.6 thousand jobs in 2024, reflecting a growth of 2.4 per cent. The informal sector created 703.7 thousand new jobs compared to 720.9 thousand in 2023, accounting for 90.0 per cent of all new jobs created, excluding small-scale agriculture."},{"p":42,"t":"In the private sector, wage employment registered a growth of 2.1 per cent in 2024 compared to 3.3 per cent in 2023. The leading industries in the private sector in 2024 providing the highest employment numbers  were  Manufacturing;  and  Agriculture, Forestry and Fishing, accounting for 15.9 per cent and 14.1 per cent, respectively. In the public sector, wage employment registered a growth of 3.1 per cent in 2024 compared to 5.9 per cent in 2023. Industries with the highest employment levels in the public sector were Education; and Public Administration and Defence; Compulsory Social Security, which accounted for 45.2 per cent and 34.4 per cent of total employment in the sector, respectively, in 2024."},{"p":42,"t":"The nominal wage bill grew by 7.2 per cent to KSh 2,998.8 billion in 2024. The private sector wage bill expanded by 7.7 per cent to KSh 2,117.4 billion in 2024, while the public sector wage bill increased by 5.8 per cent to KSh 881.4 billion in 2024, accounting for 29.4 per cent of the total wage payments in 2024."},{"p":42,"t":"Overall  average  annual  earnings  increased  from KSh 894.2 thousand in 2023 to KSh 933.1 thousand in 2024. Overall, real annual average earnings per employee decreased from KSh 667.3 thousand in 2023 to KSh 665.4 thousand in 2024."},{"p":42,"t":"The year-on-year inflation for 2024 eased to 4.5 per cent from 7.7 per cent in 2023. The inflation reported in 2024 was lowest in the last five years."},{"p":43,"t":"This was mainly due to the reduction food prices in the year under review. The annual average Consumer Price  Index  (CPI)  for  2024  stood  at  139.69. Prices of maize grain loose recorded the highest decrease of 19.3 per cent. Nairobi's lowerincome group recorded the lowest inflation of 4.2 per cent compared to other income segments in Nairobi. Overall interest rates for commercial banks increased during the review period to 16.89 per cent. The 91-Day Treasury bill interest rate declined to 10.32 per cent in December 2024 from 15.70 per cent in December 2023. Further, the CBK's inter-bank rate reduced from 11.65 per cent in December 2023 to 11.45 per cent in December 2024. Broad Money supply (M3) grew by 1.0 per cent during the review period to stand at KSh 6,105.5 billion as at December 2024. Similarly, Money supply (M2) grew by 7.8 per cent from KSh 4,496.3 billion as at December 2023 to KSh 4,845.9 billion as at end of December 2024. Quasi Money supply and M1 grew by 11.1 and 3.7 per cent, respectively during the period under review."},{"p":43,"t":"Credit advanced by commercial banks to National Government grew by 13.9 per cent from KSh 1,720.3 billion as at December 2023 to KSh 1,960.1 billion as at end of December 2024, while credit advanced to private sector declined by 1.1 per cent to KSh 4,032.0 billion as at December 2024. Total domestic credit extended by micro-finance banks decreased by 13.6 per cent to KSh 42.7 billion as at December 2024. Credit advanced by microfinance banks to the private sector declined by 15.8 per cent as at December 2024 while credit advanced to the National Government grew by 2.4 per cent to KSh 4.5 billion as at December 2024. Credit advanced by DT-SACCOs to the private sector and National Government increased by 13.4 per cent and 14.1 per cent to KSh 747.0 billion and KSh 16.2 billion, respectively as at December 2024."},{"p":43,"t":"There was a notable 34.8 per cent increase in the total value of Market capitalization from KSh 1,439.0 billion in 2023 to KSh 1,939.7 billion during the review period. Total expenditure is expected to increase from KSh 3,605.2 billion in 2023/24 to KSh 3,978.3 billion in 2024/25, resulting in a budget deficit of KSh 862.7 billion. This increase was occasioned by a 10.4 per cent rise in total export earnings and a 3.6 per cent growth in import expenditure. Total exports rose from KSh 1,007.9 billion in 2023 to KSh 1,112.3 billion in 2024, largely driven by increased re-exports. Earnings from re-exports grew by 77.3 per cent to KSh 180.2 billion in 2024, primarily due to increased re-exports of kerosene type jet fuel. Import expenditure went up from KSh 2,612.0 billion in 2023 to KSh 2,706.3 billion in 2024. Notable increases were recorded in imports of rice (23.9%), telecommunication equipment and parts (30.1%), plastics (14.3%), industrial machinery (8.0%), and aircraft  and  associated  equipment  (98.1%).  The growth in total exports, which outpaced the increase in imports, led to an improvement in the balance of trade deficit from KSh 1,604.1 billion in 2023 to KSh 1,594.0 billion in 2024. Moreover, the export to import cover ratio improved from 38.6 per cent in 2023 to 41.1 per cent in 2024."},{"p":44,"t":"The  improvement  in  general  merchandise  trade resulted in the narrowing of Current Account balance from a deficit of KSh 382.7 billion in 2023 to KSh 208.9 billion in 2024. Increased inflows from services, remittances and interest receipts further contributed to the narrowing of the Current Account deficit during 2024. There was marked increase in inflows of Reserve Assets during 2024 largely arising from increased multilateral disbursements, causing a rise in the stock of official reserves from KSh 1,145.4 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 1,303.5 billion at the end of 2024."},{"p":44,"t":"The stock of external financial assets rose from KSh 4,093.5 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 4,122.4 billion at the end of 2024. Conversely, the stock of external liabilities declined by 9.2 per cent to KSh 12,045.7 billion at the end of 2024. The projected growth is supported by easing global inflation and supply chain constraints, stronger performance in the United States and some large emerging market economies. The growth is further reinforced by improved labour market conditions, as nominal wage growth continues to show signs of moderation. However, projected global growth is  expected  to  be  uneven,  masking  significant differences across regions and countries. In advanced economies such as the USA, growth is underpinned by a robust demand reflecting strong wealth effects, easing monetary policy alongside improved financial market  conditions.  However,  in  the  euro  area, geopolitical tensions continue to weigh on growth prospects, partly due to heightened political and policy uncertainty. In other advanced economies, growth is supported by a recovery in real incomes and consumption patterns but likely hampered by uncertainty in trade policy. Growth is projected to remain stable and solid in China and India. In the Middle East and Central Asia, growth is projected to pick up in 2025 but at a slower pace. Growth in sub-Saharan Africa is expected to pick up in 2025 but expected to slow down in emerging and developing Europe."},{"p":52,"t":"Kenya's economy is projected to remain resilient and stable in 2025. The projected growth is expected to be driven by a strong services sector, enhanced agricultural productivity supported by favourable weather and distribution of subsidized fertilizer and seeds by the Government."},{"p":52,"t":"Kenya's economy is projected to remain resilient and stable in 2025. The projected growth is expected to be driven by a strong services sector, enhanced agricultural productivity supported by favourable weather and distribution  of  subsidized  fertilizer and seeds by the Government. The outlook is also underpinned by a stable macroeconomic environment and  steady  international  oil  prices.  Inflation  is projected to stay within the Government's target range of 2.5 per cent to 7.5 per cent, supported by low non-food-non-fuel inflation, lower food and energy prices, and a stable exchange rate. The decline in interest rates is expected to continue to support growth in  private  sector  credit,  boost  economic activity and accelerate growth."},{"p":52,"t":"Further, enhanced implementation of priority value chains under the Bottom-Up Economic Transformation Agenda in core areas of Agricultural Transformation; Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise (MSME) Economy; Housing and Settlement; Healthcare; and Digital Superhighway and Creative Economy- aided by the identified enablers- will accelerate inclusive growth, lower the cost of living; eradicate hunger; create jobs; expand the tax base; and improve foreign exchange balances. On the demand side, aggregate demand is expected to remain robust, supported by both public and private sector activity, stronger exports of goods and services, and sustained diaspora remittances."},{"p":53,"t":"Table 0: Summary Table"},{"p":54,"t":"Table 0: Summary Table (Cont'd)"},{"p":56,"t":"SECTION"},{"p":56,"t":"A"},{"section":"International Scene"},{"p":56,"t":"International Scene"},{"p":57,"t":"3.2%"},{"p":57,"t":"World real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew by 3.2 per cent in 2024, compared with 3.3 per cent growth recorded in 2023."},{"p":57,"t":"3.8%"},{"p":57,"t":"Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) economies grew by 3.8 per cent in 2024 compared to a growth of 3.6 per cent in 2023"},{"p":57,"t":"Economic Survey 2025"},{"section":"CHAPTER 01"},{"p":57,"t":"CHAPTER 01"},{"section":"International Scene"},{"p":57,"t":"International Scene"},{"section":"Overview"},{"p":57,"t":"Overview"},{"p":57,"t":"The global economy remained resilient in 2024 despite ongoing challenges such as high energy and food prices and tighter monetary policies. World real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew by 3.2 per cent in 2024, compared with 3.3 per cent growth recorded in 2023. The Advanced Economies seemed to catch up with the output levels projected before the pandemic while the Emerging Market and Developing Economies (EMDEs) were still bearing the scars of post COVID-19 pandemic as evidenced by a large output shortfall. The advanced Economies real GDP grew by 1.7 per cent in 2024, down from 1.8 per cent in 2023. This growth was mainly"},{"p":57,"t":"PAGE 17"},{"p":58,"t":"supported by loosening of monetary policy, strong domestic demand and increased investment. Emerging Market and Developing Economies real GDP expanded by 4.2 per cent in 2024 compared to 4.4 per cent growth in 2023. The growth was mainly supported by strong domestic demand and investment coupled with recovery in trade and exports in most of the countries. The slowdown in growth was mainly attributed to supply chain disruptions in production and shipping of commodities, especially in the oil sector.  Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) economies grew by 3.8 per cent in 2024 compared to a growth of 3.6 per cent in 2023, supported by gradual easing of supply constraints coupled with favourable weather conditions. The real GDP for the East African Community (EAC-5) region expanded by 5.4 per cent in 2024 compared to 5.3 per cent growth in 2023. The growth was mainly attributable to high government expenditure, increased productivity in the service sector and deepened interregional trade."},{"p":58,"t":"1.2. World inflation eased to 5.7 per cent in 2024 from 6.7 per cent registered in 2023. The reduction in inflation was attributed to the unwinding of the shocks such as the sharp increase in commodity prices caused by the Russia-Ukraine war, as well as improvements in the labour supply. In the advanced and emerging economies, inflation eased despite pressures in the service sector mainly due to the decline in food and energy prices. Overall inflation in the Sub-Saharan Africa however increased although most of the countries in the region recorded a reduction in inflation in 2024."},{"p":58,"t":"1.3. The world trade volume expanded by 3.4 per cent in 2024 compared to a growth of 0.7 per cent registered in 2023. The growth in global trade was buoyed by robust performance in business services and tourism. Additionally, the recovery in consumption of goods from the United States of America (USA) partly contributed to the overall improvement in global trade."},{"p":58,"t":"1.4. Global unemployment rate remained at 5.0 per cent in 2024, same as in 2023. Similarly, labour force participation rate remained at the same level of 61.0 per cent in 2024, same as what was reported in 2023. The unchanged labour force participation and unemployment rates indicated that jobs were available, but employment growth was slow."},{"section":"Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development"},{"p":58,"t":"Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development"},{"p":58,"t":"1.5. In 2024, USA economy grew by 2.8 per cent, compared to 2.9 per cent growth recorded in 2023. The economy's growth was mainly supported by strong private consumption, increased investment in industrial sectors and government spending. The growth was curtailed by subdued housing investment associated with high interest rates charged on the sector and the November 2024 elections.  Inflation eased from 3.6 per cent in 2023 to 2.4 per cent in 2024 driven by decline in energy prices and a surge in immigration which led to reduced costpush inflationary pressures. Current account balance as a percentage of GDP widened from a deficit of 3.3 per cent in 2023 to a deficit of 3.7 per cent in 2024, mainly due to decline in primary income balance coupled with global supply chain disruption attributed to labour strikes affecting ports along the East and Gulf coasts of the USA. Unemployment rate increased to 4.0 per cent in 2024 compared to 3.6 per cent recorded in 2023."},{"section":"Euro Area"},{"p":59,"t":"Euro Area"},{"p":59,"t":"1.7. The Euro Area registered real GDP growth of 0.8 per cent in 2024, an improvement from the 0.5 per cent growth recorded in 2023. Similarly, the volume of imported iron & steel and decreased by 8.9 per cent to stand at 1,101.1 thousand tonnes in 2024.In addition, the number of residential housing units completed by State Department for Housing (SDHUD) declined from 3,357 units in 2023 to 1,655 units in 2024. Transportation and storage sector grew by 4.4 per cent in 2024 compared to a growth of 5.5 per cent in 2023. The growth was evidenced by increase in consumption of light diesel, increases in port throughput, air passenger traffic and freight transport via Metre Gauge Railway (MGR). Consumption of light diesel, a key input in land transport increased from 2,163.6 thousand metric tonnes in 2023 to 2,193.0 thousand metric tonnes in 2024. Total cargo throughput handled at the port of Mombasa grew by 13.9 per cent to 40,986 thousand metric tonnes in 2024. The total passenger air traffic increased from 12,205.8 thousand passengers in 2023 to 12,831.8 thousand in 2024. Similarly, the volume of freight transported via MGR rose marginally by 2.8 per cent to 1,029 thousand tonnes in 2024. However, passengers ferried via Standard Gauge Railway (SGR) declined from 2,729 thousand passengers in 2023 to 2,447 thousand passengers in 2024. The food and non-alcoholic beverages division inflation rate increased at a slower rate of 5.6 per cent in 2024, compared to 9.7 per cent in 2023 on account increased food supply driven by favorable weather conditions. In 2024, the transport index increased by 5.0 per cent, mainly driven by the increase in prices of matatu and country bus fares, however, this rate was slower compared to 12.2 per cent in 2023. Alcoholic beverages, tobacco and narcotics division reported a 7.8 per cent inflation rate, this was mainly due to an increase in prices of miraa, cigarettes, beer, wines and spirits."},{"p":123,"t":"Table 3.15a: National Consumer Price Indices and Rates of Inflation by COICOP Divisions, 2020 - 2024"},{"p":123,"t":"Base: Feb 2019=100"},{"p":124,"t":"3.23. This was implemented through a fiscal consolidation plan aimed at reducing the fiscal deficit, slow the growth of public debt and ensure effective implementation of debt management strategy, while providing essential public services. Strategies that were developed, included enhancing revenue collection by broadening the tax base and controlling overall expenditure. Implementation of the FY 2024/25 budget will however, be affected by the withdraw -al of the Finance Bill 2024, which was expected to generate additional revenue. To ensure the smooth execution of the FY 2024/25 budget and protect the fiscal consolidation plan, ministries, departments, agencies, and counties will prioritize and rationalize expenditures while safeguarding essential government programs."},{"p":155,"t":"PAGE 115"},{"p":156,"t":"Recurrent expenditure is anticipated to grow from KSh 3,104.5 billion in 2023/24 to KSh 3,392.8 billion in 2024/25 while development expenditure is estimated to increase from KSh 500.7 billion in 2023/24 to KSh 585.5 billion in 2024/25."},{"p":156,"t":"5.2. 2023."},{"section":"KSh3,816.6B"},{"p":189,"t":"KSh3,816.6B"},{"p":189,"t":"Kenya's total merchandise trade grew by 5.5 per cent in 2024, from KSh 3,619.9 billion in 2023."},{"section":"International Trade and Balance of Payments"},{"p":189,"t":"International Trade and Balance of Payments"},{"section":"Overview"},{"p":189,"t":"Overview"},{"p":189,"t":"According to the Global Trade Update by United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), March 2025, there was a 3.7 per cent increase in global trade to USD 33 trillion in 2024. The growth was primarily driven by a 9.0 per cent increase in trade in services, contributing"},{"p":189,"t":"PAGE 149"},{"p":190,"t":"an additional USD 700 billion, while trade in goods experienced a 2.0 per cent increase in 2024. Trade in developing economies outpaced their developed counterparts, with imports and exports increasing by 4.0 per cent during the review period, mainly driven by East and South Asia. However, Africa's intra-regional trade contracted in 2024, reversing previous gains. Despite these achievements, International momentum slowed in the latter half of the year, with trade in goods growing by less than 0.5 per cent and services edging up by 1.0 per cent in the fourth quarter."},{"p":190,"t":"6.2. Kenya's total merchandise trade amounted to KSh 3,818.6 billion in 2024, reflecting a growth of 5.5 per cent compared to KSh 3,619.9 billion in 2023. This increase was driven by a 10.4 per cent rise in export earnings, which amounted to KSh 1,112.3 billion in 2024, up from KSh 1,007.9 billion in 2023. The growth in exports was largely attributed to a 77.3 per cent surge in re-exports, while domestic exports recorded a 2.9 per cent increase. However, imports grew at a slower pace from KSh 2,612.0 billion in 2023 to KSh 2,706.3 billion in 2024. This resulted in an improved trade balance from a deficit of KSh 1,604.1 billion in 2023 to a deficit of KSh 1,594.0 billion in 2024."},{"p":190,"t":"6.3. Table 6.17 highlights Kenya's international investment position for 2020 to 2024. The stock of external assets has exhibited an increasing trend over time and rose from KSh 4,093.5 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 4,122.4 billion at the end of 2024. These assets were mainly in the form of other investments which accounted for 32.9 per cent of the total stock of assets at KSh 1,357.0 billion at the end of 2024. The bulk of these assets were in currency and deposits, and loans advanced by Deposit Taking Corporations in Kenya. The stock of reserve assets was the second major category of assets, rising from KSh 1,145.4 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 1,301.5 billion at the end of 2024. This build up was on account of increased disbursements from multilateral sources, forex earnings from exports of agricultural commodities and remittance inflows in 2024. Holdings of Portfolio assets continued to increase from KSh 897.6 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 1,028.0 billion at the end of 2024, mainly driven by an increase in holdings of equity and debt assets by other sectors. Similarly, the stock of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) assets rose by 15.1 per cent to KSh 428.9 billion at the end of 2024."},{"p":219,"t":"6.42. The stock of external liabilities declined by 9.2 per cent to KSh 12,045.7 billion at the end of 2024. These liabilities were majorly in Other Investments, which accounted for 76.9 per cent of the total stock of external liabilities at the end of 2024. Despite this category accounting for the bulk of external liabilities, there was 11.0 per cent decline in the stock of liabilities to KSh 9,268.7 billion, partly attributable to a decrease in the stock of loans to General Government and Other Sectors at the end of 2024. On the contrary, the stock of other accounts payable continued to increase and amounted to KSh 2,985.6 billion at the end of 2024. Portfolio Investment liabilities accounted for 10.3 per cent of the stock of external liabilities in 2024 at KSh 1,240.7 billion. Debt securities constitute most of Portfolio liabilities and declined from KSh 1,311.4 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 1,188.4 billion at the end of 2024 due to debt servicing of the International Sovereign Bond. Consequently, the stock of external liabilities of General Government, which largely constituted loans and debt securities, declined from KSh 5,707.4 billion at the end of 2023 to KSh 4,893.0 billion at the end of 2024."},{"p":219,"t":"6.43. In 2024, the Kenyan Shilling showed signs of recovery against major international currencies, as indicated by the Trade Weighted Index (TWI), which improved from 138.25 in 2023 to 129.99 in 2024. Table 7.1 presents agriculture output and intermediate consumption from 2020 to 2024.The Agriculture Gross Value Added (GVA) expanded by 4.4 per cent to KSh 1,706.0 billion in the period under review. The Food Balance Sheet (FBS) presents a systematic and coherent structure for analyzing the total food supply and use in a country over a given reference period. It compiles data on domestic food production, imports, exports, stock changes, and various types of food usage. The FBS generates crucial indicators including the Import Dependency Ratio (IDR) and Self-Sufficiency Ratio (SSR) at the aggregate"},{"section":"FBS Indicators"},{"p":249,"t":"FBS Indicators"},{"p":249,"t":"7.28. As shown in Table 7.22 and Figure 7.4, the FBS results indicate that in 2024, per caput daily caloric supply increased to 2,152.7 thousand calories, while protein supplies recorded a 1.6 per cent increase to 64.0 grams per person. Vegetal products remain the dominant source of calories, with cereals contributing approximately 942.8 thousand calories per caput daily. Caloric supply from animal products increased from 225.7 thousand calories in 2023 to 227.8 thousand"},{"p":249,"t":"Table 7.22: Food Balance Sheet, 2020-2024"},{"p":249,"t":"levels. It plays a key role in calculating per capita food supply values, including calories, proteins, and fats. The tool serves as an essential tool for policymakers and stakeholders, facilitating evidence-based decision-making to enhance agricultural performance and ensure national food security. The FBS depicts Kenya's overall food situation, offering insights into the trends over a period of five years."},{"p":249,"t":"calories in 2024. 2025"},{"section":"CHAPTER 08"},{"p":259,"t":"CHAPTER 08"},{"section":"Environment and Natural Resources"},{"p":259,"t":"Environment and Natural Resources"},{"section":"Overview"},{"p":259,"t":"Overview"},{"p":259,"t":"The Environment and Natural Resources sector plays a crucial role in Kenya's economic development,  providing  the  foundation  for key industries such as agriculture, tourism, and energy. In the face of climate change, population growth, and rapid urbanization, the management of these resources is vital for ensuring sustainable development and improving the quality of life for all Kenyans. The Government of Kenya (GoK)"},{"p":259,"t":"PAGE 219"},{"p":260,"t":"has actively mobilized both domestic and international resources to support climate mitigation and adaptation, implementing legislative frameworks and strategic partnerships to build climate resilience and transition toward a low-carbon economy. One of Kenya's flagship initiatives is the Financing Locally Led Climate Action (FLLoCA) Programme which is implemented with support from the World Bank with a focus on performance-based funding to ensure accountability and funding in climate change interventions. In 2024, Kenya submitted its first Bi -ennial Transparency Report (BTR) under the Paris Agreement as required by the enhanced Transparency Framework."},{"p":260,"t":"8.2. During the year under review, the Environment and Natural Resource sector recorded mixed performances across several key indicators. The total development expenditure on water supply and related services is expected to reduce by 4.1 per cent to KSh 64.3 billion in 2024/25. The number of water purification points (WPPs) is projected to increase from 376 in 2023/24 to 380 in 2024/25. The total quantity of fish landed increased by 4.4 per cent to 168.4 thousand tonnes in 2024. Overall, the total value of fish landed increased from KSh 35.9 billion in 2023 to KSh 39.6 billion in 2024. The net stocked plantation area increased from 144.8 thousand hectares in 2023 to 147.0 thousand hectares in 2024. The total earnings from mineral production recorded a decline of 24.4 per cent from KSh 33.8 billion in 2023 to KSh 25.5 billion in 2024. The long rain season March-April-May (MAM) was characterized by above-average rainfall across most parts of the country."},{"section":"Environment and Natural Resources Gross Value Added"},{"p":260,"t":"Environment and Natural Resources Gross Value Added"},{"p":260,"t":"8.3. The Gross Value Added from the Environment and Natural Resources sector increased by 16.0 per cent to KSh 632.0 billion in 2024. The contribution of the Environment and Natural resources sector to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) was 3.9 per cent in 2024."},{"p":260,"t":"The Gross Value Added from the Environment and Natural Resources sector increased by 16.0 per cent to KSh 632.0 billion in 2024."},{"p":260,"t":"Table 8.1: Trends in Environment and Natural Resources Gross Value Added, 2020- 2024"},{"p":260,"t":"KSh Million"},{"section":"Water Supply"},{"p":261,"t":"Water Supply"},{"p":261,"t":"8.4. Rainfall in 2024 exhibited significant spatial and temporal variability. April and November recorded the highest totals, with widespread and localized flash flooding in the central highlands, including the Nai -robi area in April. The western and central highlands experienced above-normal rainfall, while most other regions saw drier-than-average conditions."},{"p":273,"t":"8.27. The March-April-May (MAM) season was characterized by above-average rainfall across most parts of the country, with some areas receiving 111-200% of the long-term mean (LTM). Central Kenya and the Rift Valley recorded rainfall exceeding 200% of the average. However, the Coastal region, Lake Victoria Basin, and parts of western Kenya experienced near to below-average rainfall, with the Coastal region"},{"section":"Number"},{"p":273,"t":"Number"},{"p":273,"t":"recording less than 90% of normal levels."},{"p":273,"t":"8.28. The highest seasonal rainfall total was recorded at Dagoretti Corner station (1,353.3mm) and Wilson Airport (1,097.1mm) in Nairobi County.  Other stations, including Kericho (805.6mm), Machakos (830.2mm), JKIA (840.1mm), Kisii (866.6mm), Kakamega (869.8mm), Embu (955.7mm) and Thika (985.9mm) recorded over 800mm of rainfall. Nyahururu, Moi Air Base, Dagoretti Corner, Wilson Airport, JKIA, Kabete, Thika, and Machakos, experienced their wettest MAM season on record. Wajir (153.8 millimeters) and Garissa (184.4 millimeters) record the lowest rainfall during the season. The MAM Long Rains seasonal rainfall analysis is shown in Figure 8.2."},{"p":274,"t":"Figure 8.2: Long Rains (March-April-May) Performance, 2024"},{"p":274,"t":"Source: Kenya Meteorological Department"},{"p":274,"t":"8.29. The October-November-December (OND) 'Short Rains' season is a crucial rainfall period in Kenya, particularly for the Central and Eastern regions. October typically marks the onset of the short rains across many parts of the country, while November is generally the peak month of the season. December signals the cessation of the short rains, except in southern Kenya near the Tanzanian border, where rains normally persist for longer."},{"p":274,"t":"8.30. The predictability of the OND season is influenced  by  two  primary  climate  drivers:  the  El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) and the Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD). However, forecasting Sea Surface Temperatures (SSTs) beyond the March-May period presents challenges due to the spring predictability barrier. As a result, it remains crucial to monitor updates issued by the meteorological department"},{"p":274,"t":"for more accurate seasonal outlooks and potential changes in climatic conditions."},{"p":274,"t":"8.31. The rainfall distribution during the OND season was generally poor over most parts of the country as shown in Figure 8.3. Kakamega Meteorological Station (619.2 millimeters), Kericho (583.4 millimeters), Kisii Meteorological stations (571.8 millimeters) and Dagoretti weather station (540.6 millimeters) recorded the highest amount of rainfall respectively."},{"p":274,"t":"8.32. The MAM 2024 season marked the Long Rains periods that led to devastating floods, causing loss of lives, widespread destruction of crops, property, and livelihoods, displacement of populations and loss of livestock. The MAM 2024 started barely a few months into the recovery phase following the OND-2023 El Niño enhanced rainfall."},{"p":277,"t":"8.36. The enhanced MAM 2024 Long Rains resulted in flooding that impacted most parts of the country as predicted by the Kenya Meteorological Department (KMD). A total of 315 people died due to the floods, 58,641 households of which 293,205 individuals were displaced, and 412,263 persons were affect -ed. The flooding caused damage to roads, bridges, aerodromes, railway lines, school infrastructure, and health facilities where 298,673 acres of agricultural land and 9,367 livestock were washed away."},{"p":277,"t":"8.37. In the education sector, a total of 1,203 primary schools with a pupil population of 439,081 were affected, where 3,945 toilets were sunken, and 233 schools had their roofs blown off. At the same time, 294 secondary schools with a population of 124,358 students were affected, 941 toilets were sunken, and 81 schools had their roofs blown off. Over 48 schools hosted IDPs in various Counties affected by floods."},{"p":277,"t":"8.38: The KMD issued nine (9) Heavy Rainfall, Strong Winds and Large Waves advisories along with other daily, weekly and monthly updates as part of the early warning information distributed across the country. The 29 th  United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP29) was held in Baku, Azerbaijan in November 2024. The conference, dubbed the 'Climate Finance COP,' emphasized the necessity for innovative and equitable funding mechanisms to bolster global adaptation and resilience efforts. The need for de -veloped nations to fulfill financial commitments to support developing countries was discussed, revealing a significant funding gap as only $250 billion is pledged annually against the $1.3 trillion required for climate action in developing nations. In response, the Government of Kenya (GoK) has actively mobilized both domestic and international resources to support climate mitigation and adaptation, implementing legislative frameworks and strategic partnerships to build climate resilience and transition towards a low-carbon economy."},{"p":280,"t":"8.42. The Financing Locally Led Climate Action (FLLoCA) Programme, implemented with support from the World Bank, focuses on performance-based funding to ensure accountability and impact. Additionally, the Climate Change Act (2016) established the Climate Change Fund, requiring counties to set up County Climate Change Funds (CCCFs) for investments in renewable energy, afforestation, and disaster risk management. Kenya has also embraced green financ -ing instruments, such as green bonds listed on the Nairobi Securities Exchange (NSE), and is developing a carbon credit trading system to enhance private sector participation in climate action."},{"p":280,"t":"8.43. To further boost climate finance, Kenya has secured funding from global institutions, including the Green Climate Fund (GCF), Adaptation Fund, and Climate Investment Funds (CIFs). The government is also promoting Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) through organizations like KEPSA, attracting investments in clean energy, reforestation, and sustainable infrastructure. The National Climate Change Action Plan (NCCAP) serves as the guiding framework, aligning Kenya's climate investments with Vision 2030 and the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), focusing on renewable energy expansion, climate-smart agriculture, and disaster risk management."},{"p":280,"t":"8.44: In 2024, Kenya submitted its first Biennial Trans -parency Report (BTR) under the Paris Agreement as required by the enhanced Transparency Framework. The report comprehensively describes climate actions that Kenya has implemented, is implementing, and plans to implement to achieve its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) and the global long-term goals of the Paris Agreement."},{"p":280,"t":"8.45: The Biennial Transparency Report includes national inventory report on anthropogenic Green House Gas (GHG) emissions and removals, information necessary to track progress in implementing and achieving NDCs under Article 4 of the Paris Agreement, information related to climate change impacts and adaptation under Article 7 of the Paris Agreement, and information on financial, technology transfer and capacity-building support needed and received from developed country Parties under Articles 9, 10 and 11 of the Paris Agreement."},{"section":"20.9%"},{"p":281,"t":"20.9%"},{"p":281,"t":"The quantity of imported petroleum products rose by 20.9 per cent from 4.3 million tonnes in 2023 to 5.2 million tonnes in 2024."},{"section":"5.1%"},{"p":281,"t":"5.1%"},{"p":281,"t":"Total electricity demand expanded by 5.1 per cent to 14,101.9 GWh in 2024. There was growth in electricity demand for all categories except street lighting, whose demand declined by 17.6 per cent to 89.9 GWh in 2024. Demand for large and medium (commercial and industrial),  and  domestic  and  small  commercial categories increased by 4.1 per cent and 4.9 per cent to 5,515.2 GWh and 4,459.1 GWh, respectively. Similarly, demand for rural electrification increased by 3.9 per cent to 684.79 GWh in 2024. E-mobility was introduced in the review period with a demand of 2.8 GWh. Local electricity generation increased by 65 GWh to 12,569.3 GWh in 2024. Electricity exports increased by 26.6 per cent to 42.8 GWh in 2024. Electricity transmission and distributive losses were 3,307.4 GWh accounting for 23.5 per cent of total electricity supply in the review period."},{"p":292,"t":"Table 9.10: Electricity Demand and Supply, 2020-2024"},{"p":292,"t":"Source: Kenya Power and Lighting Company Ltd"},{"section":"Notes:"},{"p":292,"t":"Notes:"},{"p":292,"t":"5.1%"},{"p":292,"t":"Total electricity demand increased by 5.1 per cent to 14,101.9 GWh in 2024. Environmental Economic Accounts are systems used to account for natural resource utilization. The accounts take stock of natural resources from extraction  to  intermediate  use  by  industries  to final use, to residuals/waste, which are eventually disposed of back to the environment. In addition, the  accounts  seek  to  promote  efficient  natural resource accounting and ensure a country can track how much it has utilized, estimate reserves in the environment, and promote proper disposal of residuals for  environmental  sustainability.  The  System  of Environmental Economic Accounts (SEEA) framework follows a similar accounting structure as the System of National Accounts (SNA). The concepts, definitions, and  classifications  used  in  SEEA  are  consistent with the 2008 SNA to facilitate the integration of environmental and economic statistics."},{"section":"Energy Balance, 2024"},{"p":294,"t":"Energy Balance, 2024"},{"p":294,"t":"9.22. The manufacturing sector therefore plays a vital role in advancing the country's socio-economic development by driving job creation, attracting foreign direct investment and boosting foreign exchange earnings. The sector's real value added grew by 2.8 per cent in 2024, an improvement from the 2.2 per cent recorded in 2023, reinforcing the sectors' role in economic transformation. In the year under review the manufacturing sector's share of GDP stood at 7.3 per cent. The sector's output expanded by 4.4 per cent in 2024 up from a growth of 2.1 per cent in 2023, mainly fueled by the manufacture of food products."},{"p":301,"t":"PAGE 261"},{"p":302,"t":"10.2. The agro-based industries recorded a 9.8 per cent growth in 2024, up from the 1.2 per cent growth registered in 2023. Other key notable growths included Dairy Products at 11.5 per cent, Prepared and Preserved Fruits and Vegetables at 8.8 per cent; Bakery Products at 7.8 per cent, and Animal and Vegetable Fats and Oils at 3.3 per cent. This growth aligns with the commitment to bolstering food security and fostering value addition."},{"p":302,"t":"10.3. Road development plays a crucial role in enhancing connectivity,  promoting  economic  growth,  and facilitating  trade  and  mobility.  Kenya  has  given priority to investment in road infrastructure, with significant budgetary allocations aimed at upgrading trunk and primary roads, improving secondary and minor roads; and maintaining existing networks. Table 11.9 shows the Status of ongoing R2000 and R10000 Programme for the Period, 2020-2024. The Roads 'R2000' and 'R10000 programme' focuses on Low Volume Sealed Roads (LVSR) upgrading to bitumen standard, which remains a key pillar in Kenya's infrastructure development strategy. This initiative aims to improve connectivity, enhance rural- urban integration, and boost economic activities by upgrading gravel and earth roads to bitumen standard. During the year under review, there were roadworks in all counties except Lamu County. A total of 2,456.7 kilometres were maintained or graded at an estimated cost of KSh 255.2 billion."},{"p":324,"t":"Table 11.9: Status of ongoing R2000 and R10000 Programme for the Period, 2020-2024"},{"p":325,"t":"Table 11.9: Status of ongoing R2000 and R10000 Programme for the Period, 2020-2024 (Continued)"},{"p":326,"t":"11.12. The number of cybersecurity advisories and reported online crime experienced significant changes in the period under review as shown in Table 14.15. The total cybersecurity advisories increased by 48.1 per cent to 39.0 million in 2024.   Malware advisories more than doubled from 1.1 million in 2023 to 2.5 million in 2024, while website application attack advisories more than tripled from about 3.9 million in 2023 to 13.6 million in 2024. Conversely, advisories related to system vulnerabilities declined by 11.3 per cent while those for botnet/DDoS attacks dropped by 28.0 per cent in 2024.  It is important to note that the reporting of digital investigation and digital forensics advisories was decommissioned in 2024 due to change of reporting of these crimes."},{"p":389,"t":"14.30. The total number of reported online crimes almost doubled to 3.5 billion in 2024. During the review period, two new categories of online crimes were reported. These were Brute force attacks which involve automated, systematic attempts to crack passwords or encryption by trying every possible combination until the correct one is found and mobile application attacks which target vulnerabilities in mobile apps to compromise user data or disrupt services. In 2024, 127.8 million Brute force attacks and 526.4 thousand mobile application attacks were reported."},{"p":390,"t":"Table 14.15: Cyber Security Advisories and Reported Online Crimes, 2020 - 2024"},{"p":390,"t":"Source: Communication Authority of Kenya"},{"section":"Electronic Waste"},{"p":390,"t":"Electronic Waste"},{"p":390,"t":"14.31. Electronic waste (E -waste) is calculated using the E-waste toolkit which tracks the annual flow of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) on imports coming into the country, exports leaving the country and goods manufactured domestically. Following the methodology of the ITU's E -waste Statistics Partnership for ICT Development, these figures are combined with the typical service life of each product to predict when the equipment will reach end -of -life and become waste."},{"p":390,"t":"The total E-waste generated in the five-year peri- od increased from 46,211 metric tonnes, in 2020 to 53,559 metric tonnes in 2024 as presented in Table 14.16.  Large equipment (excluding photovoltaic panels) E-waste increased by 9.9 per cent to 5,680 metric tonnes in 2024. Small equipment E-waste increased by 5.6 per cent to 19,737 metric tonnes and accounted for 36.9 per cent of the total E-waste generated in 2024. However, E-waste for lamps and small IT and telecommunication equipment decreased by 3.9 per cent and 2.3 per cent to 4,844 metric tonnes and 6,581 metric tonnes, respectively, in the review period."},{"p":391,"t":"Table 14.16: Electronic Waste Generated, 2020 - 2024"},{"section":"*Provisional"},{"p":391,"t":"*Provisional"},{"section":"Konza Technopolis"},{"p":391,"t":"Konza Technopolis"},{"p":391,"t":"14.32. Konza Technopolis, the flagship smart city, stands as a key pillar in the country's journey towards becoming a knowledge-based economy. As one of the core enablers under BETA, specifically the Digital Superhighway pillar, Konza is not only a symbol of technological advancement but also a strategic platform for delivering innovation, generating high-value jobs and driving inclusive socio-economic transformation. The project is anchored on a robust blend of horizontal and vertical infrastructure. Horizontal infrastructure refers to the foundational, ground -level networks roads, utility corridors, water and sewer lines, power distribution, and fibre -optic duct while vertical infrastructure comprises the above ground structures such as office towers, data centres, residential blocks and mixed-use complexes. The key performing indicators for the Technopolis are intended to support evidence-based policymaking by informing data-driven decisions and enhancing transparency and accountability. Table 14.17 highlights selected indicators, which were identified based on Konza's strategic priorities and aligned with the intended outcomes of the Fourth Medium Term Plan (MTP IV)."},{"p":391,"t":"14.33. The number of investors in the Technopolis rose to 70 in 2024, from 56 in 2023, reflecting sus -tained confidence in its value proposition as a smart city and innovation hub. Consequently, the annual amount invested grew by 27.3 per cent to KSh 13.6 billion in 2024 which led to KSh 83.5b cumulative investment in the Technopolis as at the end of 2024"},{"p":391,"t":"Metric tonnes"},{"p":392,"t":"14.34. Revenue generated from Konza Technopolis amounted to KSh 252.4 million in 2024, from KSh 191.3 million in 2023. This growth was largely attributable to revenue from the Konza Cloud, which rose more than four times to KSh 151.6 million in 2024. The surge underscores the growing demand for cloud infrastructure and services within Kenya's innovation ecosystem. Conversely, revenue from land lease declined by 17.4 per cent to KSh 76.1 million, while other revenue streams declined by 60.9 per cent to KSh 24.7 million in 2024."},{"p":392,"t":"14.35. Utilization of Konza Cloud resources has intensified over the past five years. Available storage increased to 25.0 per cent in 2024 from 22.0 per cent in  2023,  after  additional  disks  were  commissioned, which pushed the share of used storage down to 75.0 per cent in 2024 from 78.0 per cent in 2023. The proportion of idle virtual CPUs fell to 62.0 per cent in 2024 from 71.0 per cent in 2023, signaling more intensive workloads on the platform."},{"p":392,"t":"14.36. Under the smart city facilities, the number of smart poles installed increased by 35.0 per cent to 81 in 2024. These smart poles, equipped with features such as surveillance cameras, Wi-Fi access points, and environmental sensors, are instrumental in enhancing urban safety, connectivity, and efficiency. Completion of the Intelligent Operations Centre (IOC), a critical facility for real-time monitoring and management of city services, advanced substantially to 82.0 per cent in 2024, up from 35.0 per cent the previous year."},{"p":392,"t":"In addition, the length of fibre optics laid increased to 79 kilometers in 2024 from 50 kilometers in 2023. Junction signalization doubled, to 12 intersections, which is expected to improve traffic flow and smart mobility."},{"p":392,"t":"14.37. The Jitume Digital Program continued to strengthen Konza Technopolis' role as a catalyst for digital skills development and youth empowerment in the period under review.  The number of digital hubs equipped with Virtual Digital Infrastructure (VDIs) rose from 109 in 2023 to 193 in 2024, while the number of VDI units deployed including monitors, smart boards, virtual desktops, webcams, routers and digital conferencing tools increased by 53.3 per cent to 15,186 in 2024."},{"p":392,"t":"14.38. The number of parcels surveyed rose from 187 in 2023 to 515 in 2024, while the number of parcels leased grew more than threefold from 9 in 2023 to 33 in 2024 signaling renewed investor interest in securing space within the smart city. The Social Health Insurance (SHI) Act 2023 became operational in October 2023, repealing the NHIF Act of 1998. Consequently, the Social Health Authority (SHA) was established by the SHI Act 2023 to manage three primary funds, namely: Primary Health care (PHC) Fund, Social Health Insurance Fund (SHIF) and Emergency, Chronic and Critical Illness Fund to enhance healthcare financing in Kenya. The commencement date for the Act was 22 nd  November 2023, after gazettement by the Cabinet Secretary for Health, with a one-year transition which ended on 21 st  November 2024."},{"p":471,"t":"16.39. The Social Health Insurance (SHI) Act 2023 aims to: promote strategic purchasing of health care services; enhance the pooling of resources and risk based on  the principles of solidarity, equity and efficiency to guarantee access to health care services to all; provide a framework for improved health outcomes and financial protection; and realign health care systems, process and programs for responsiveness, reliability and sustainability of health care in Kenya. Social Health Insurance is guided by the principles of pooling financial resources and risks -everyone contributing the same proportion of their income, everyone getting a standard package of benefits and standard reimbursement to all types of facilities."},{"p":471,"t":"16.40. Registration of citizens to SHA began on 1 st July 2024 while the roll out was from 1 st  October 2024. Central to SHA is Taifa Care which denotes the whole Universal Health Coverage (UHC) journey that includes all the pillars that constitute delivery of quality health services to all Kenyans."},{"section":"Appendix 16.1: Outpatient Diseases reported in health facilities"},{"p":472,"t":"Appendix 16.1: Outpatient Diseases reported in health facilities"},{"section":"101,220"},{"p":473,"t":"101,220"},{"p":473,"t":"The total number of crimes reported decreased by 3.6 per cent to 101,220 in 2024."},{"section":"16 Billion"},{"p":473,"t":"16 Billion"},{"p":473,"t":"The value of public assets traced by EACC increased from KSh 6.6 billion in 2023 to KSh 16.0 billion in 2024."},{"p":473,"t":"Economic Survey 2025"},{"section":"CHAPTER 17"},{"p":473,"t":"CHAPTER 17"},{"section":"Governance, Peace and Security"},{"p":473,"t":"Governance, Peace and Security"},{"section":"Overview"},{"p":473,"t":"Overview"},{"p":473,"t":"PAGE 433 Governance, Peace and Security statistics are critical in determining the success of economic policies or major factors influencing the level of national development and the well-being of the population. Similarly, NSSF contributions more than doubled to KSh 59.3 billion in 2024 while the benefits paid increased by 45.5 per cent to KSh 9.7 billion in 2024."},{"p":532,"t":"Table 18.16: National Social Security Fund Annual Membership, Contributions and Benefits by Sex, 2020-2024"},{"p":532,"t":"Source: National Social Security Fund"},{"p":532,"t":"* Provisional"},{"p":532,"t":"1 The reduction in employer registration in the year 2022 was due to partial shutdown of the intergation process between NSSF and KRA when the (Business Registration Service (BRS) portal was out for nearly 6 months"},{"p":532,"t":"18.23. In particular, it is used by the Central Banks as a measure of price stability which promotes long-term planning, investment and economic growth. In general, the primary objective of Central Banks is to formulate and implement monetary policy with the aim of achieving and maintaining stability in the general"},{"p":537,"t":"PAGE 497"},{"p":538,"t":"maintaining stability in the general level of prices in the economy. In Kenya, the Central Bank of Kenya (CBK) implements monetary policy with the aim of achieving a medium-term inflation target of 5.0 per cent, with an allowance of ± 2.5 per cent deviation in the short term."},{"p":538,"t":"19.2. According to the East African Monetary Union (EAMU) Protocol, Partner States' macroeconomic convergence criteria require a performance ceiling on headline inflation as well as an indicative one on core inflation. Therefore, the need for a consistent measure of core inflation in line with broad East Af -rica Community (EAC) guidelines is vital. Secondly, core inflation plays an important role in informing monetary policy particularly under Inflation Target -ing framework. Central banks, especially inflation targeting ones, use measures of core inflation in their communication to the public."},{"p":538,"t":"19.3. Previously,  the  CBK  generated  Non-Food Non-Fuel (NFNF) as a proxy measure of core infla -tion using Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS) data. The NFNF was computed by excluding food, transport and energy components from the overall inflation. While this measure played a vital role in understanding underlying inflation dynamics, it be -came imperative to have an official comprehensive measure of core inflation for Kenya. This need was informed by the fact that CBK has been transitioning to Inflation Targeting monetary policy framework and recently officially adopted the framework in line with the White Paper on Modernization of the Monetary Policy Framework and Operations, July 2021. While"},{"p":539,"t":"price stability remains the main objective, distinguishing between headline inflation and core/underlying inflation is not only crucial but also a standard practice under Inflation Targeting framework. The transition to inflation targeting among many countries including developing and low-income countries in Africa is partly driven by the drawbacks of targeting monetary aggregates and the rapid evolution of financial innovations."},{"p":539,"t":"19.4. The KNBS has been publishing headline inflation at the end of every month without disaggregating it into core and non-core inflation. In January 2025, the Bureau started publishing Core and Non-Core Inflation. Core inflation is a measure of inflation that excludes commodities in the Consumer Price Index (CPI) basket whose prices are highly volatile and is critical in informing monetary policy decisions since it captures persistent price developments consistent with macroeconomic fundamentals. Specifically, core inflation provides an indi -cation of demand side pressures. Prices of the excluded components (non-core) are often driven by transient or supply-side factors such as seasonal weather conditions and international oil prices."},{"section":"Core and Non-Core Inflations"},{"p":539,"t":"Core and Non-Core Inflations"},{"p":539,"t":"19.5. Core inflation is a measure of inflation that is com -piled from products whose prices are non-volatile such as manufactured food items, health services, education services and ICT. The concept of core inflation is to re -move most or all the products whose prices are highly volatile or transient from the overall index since they cause distortions to the overall index. By removing such products from the index, it is argued that the remaining, less volatile index, better demonstrates the fundamental changes in the rate of inflation. It can thus be used for economic analysis and, indeed, economic policy decisions, particularly by the Central Bank whose primary objective is to ensure price stability."},{"p":539,"t":"19.6. Non-core inflation on the other hand is a measure that is compiled from products whose prices are volatile such as fresh vegetables, fresh milk, electricity, petrol, diesel, kerosene and bus fares. Prices of these commodities are influenced by supply-side factors such as seasonal weather patterns and fluctuations in inter -national oil prices."},{"section":"Methods of Computing Core Inflation Measures"},{"p":539,"t":"Methods of Computing Core Inflation Measures"},{"p":539,"t":"19.7. Literature identifies three main approaches to core inflation computation: exclusion method, statis -tical methods (including trimmed mean and weighted median among others) and model-based approach. The EAC convergence criteria guidelines recommends the exclusion approach to be used in the computation of"},{"p":539,"t":"Core inflation is a measure of inflation that is compiled from products whose prices are non-volatile such as manufactured food items, health services, education services and ICT. Non-core inflation on the other hand is  a  measure that is compiled from products  whose  prices  are  volatile such as fresh vegetables, fresh milk, electricity, petrol, diesel, kerosene and bus fares."},{"p":539,"t":"the core inflation in the region. In lines with this recom -mendation, Kenya adopted the exclusion method which excludes items with historically volatile prices, such as food and energy, from the overall CPI. This method highlights persistent inflation by eliminating items with price fluctuations. The approach is criticized for its `ad hoc` method of including or excluding components in core inflation calculations. Excluding all food and energy items may result in the loss of valuable information, as not all of these components show excess volatility. Retained components may include items whose prices are volatile that bias core inflation measures. Consequently, some central banks use multiple measures of core inflation alongside the exclusion-based approach."},{"p":540,"t":"19.8. The statistical approach includes two main methods: trimmed mean and weighted median. The trimmed mean excludes extreme price changes (outliers) at a 10 per cent rate. The weighted median approach (50% trim) measures core inflation by focusing on the price change at the 50 th  percentile of the monthly distribution, aiming to capture central price changes and mitigate the impact of large fluctuations. However, the trimmed mean method requires some technical consideration:"},{"p":540,"t":"(i) whether the trim will be symmetric or asymmetric;"},{"p":540,"t":"(ii) choice of frequency of price changes;"},{"p":540,"t":"(iii) seasonality; and"},{"p":540,"t":"(iv) treatment of large expenditure items such as housing with large weights in the CPI."},{"p":540,"t":"19.9. Frequent changes in technical decisions can lead to reputation and communication challenges for the Central Bank, as they impact core inflation measures. The"},{"p":540,"t":"model-based approach employs a common component method to determine core inflation using dynamic factor models from the CPI basket. This approach is rarely used by central banks, but literature shows that the US Federal Reserve and Bank of Canada employ factor models for alternative core inflation measures. The effectiveness of these measures for monetary policy relies on how well they enhance the transparency and accountability of the central bank."},{"p":540,"t":"19.10. The EAC Guidelines for Compiling Harmonized Consumer Price Indices (HCPI), March 2022 recommends exclusion of all items under the following broad categories in the computation of core indices: fresh fruits; fresh vegetables; fresh milk; dried vegetables; tobacco leaves; imported fuels (e.g., petrol, diesel, paraffin, and cooking gas etc.); solid fuels (e.g., charcoal, firewood etc.); and utilities (electricity, metered water, and unmetered water). Table 19.1 list the products under Classification of Individual Consumption by Purpose (COICOP 2018) recommended for exclusion in the core basket."},{"p":541,"t":"Table 19.1: Products Recommended from Exclusion in Core Basket by EAC"},{"section":"Selection of the Core and Non-Core Baskets and Weights"},{"p":542,"t":"Selection of the Core and Non-Core Baskets and Weights"},{"p":542,"t":"19.11. The process of computing a core inflation measure for Kenya followed a two-step procedure. In the first step, the EAC guidelines for computation of harmonized CPIs for the EAC countries were used to identify the items to be excluded. This generated an initial core CPI basket of 280 items with a weight of 83.1 per cent. However, in addition to the list of recommended products, each EAC Partner State is supposed to determine additional CPI items to be excluded during the computation of the core index, based on measures of persistence and volatility of monthly consumer prices. Therefore, in the second step, robust statistical methods of assessing volatility such as standard deviations were applied to determine additional items in the CPI basket to be included/ excluded from the computation of the core inflation basket. Thus, the commodity indices were subjected to these statistical measures and a selection criterion was determined whereby commodities with a standard deviation above 25 were considered volatile and were excluded from the core CPI basket. For robustness, the standard deviations were computed over"},{"p":542,"t":"Table 19.2: Core CPI Weights by COICOP Divisions"},{"p":542,"t":"different time periods, while excluding outlier years that were characterized with severe shocks such as COVID-19 pandemic and drought. Consequently, using historical trends, standard deviations and coefficient of variations, five additional items were identified and excluded in the computation of core inflation for Kenya. Therefore, the total core CPI basket constitutes 275 items and has a weight of 81.1 per cent. This is a more representative basket for the computation of core inflation compared with NFNF proxy measure of core inflation previously computed by CBK."},{"section":"Core Basket and Weights"},{"p":542,"t":"Core Basket and Weights"},{"p":542,"t":"19.12. To calculate the core inflation, a core CPI sub-basket is derived from the elementary CPI dataset of 330 items that is used in computing the headline CPI. After generation of the core CPI sub-basket, core inflation is then calculated based on annual, monthly or quarterly changes in the sub-basket index. The share of the core CPI sub-basket is 81.1 per cent as presented in Table 19.2."},{"p":543,"t":"19.13. The proxy for core inflation previ -ously used by the CBK, i.e. the NFNF inflation, had 200 items which constitutes 53.4 per cent of the overall CPI basket. Hence, the new measure of core inflation constitutes a larger share of the overall CPI basket and thus, provides a better capture of inflation dynamics."},{"section":"Non-Core Basket and Weights"},{"p":543,"t":"Non-Core Basket and Weights"},{"p":543,"t":"19.14. The non-core CPI index comprises goods and services whose prices are susceptible to short-term shocks and are, therefore, volatile. It is computed from the elementary goods and services items excluded from the CPI basket in the computation of the core index bringing the total number of items in the non-core index to 55. These items constitute a weight of 18.9 per cent of the overall CPI basket as presented in Table 19.3."},{"p":543,"t":"Table 19.3: Summary of Weights for Core and Non-Core Indices"},{"section":"Sub-Categories of the Non-Core Index"},{"p":543,"t":"Sub-Categories of the Non-Core Index"},{"p":543,"t":"19.15.
