Surveys"},{"p":557,"t":"Highlights of the 2023/24 Kenya Housing and Real Estate Surveys"},{"section":"Overview"},{"p":557,"t":"Overview"},{"p":557,"t":"This  section  provides  highlights  of  the 2023/24  Kenya  Housing  Survey  (KHS). The Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS)  conducted  the  2023/24  KHS  to update statistics on housing and residential dwellings in Kenya necessary for informing housing  sector  policies,  compilation  of national accounts statistics and international comparisons. Given that"},{"p":557,"t":"PAGE 517"},{"p":558,"t":"the last National Housing Survey was conducted in 2012, it was imperative to undertake a new survey to update the data on housing and fill data gaps necessary for periodic monitoring of the sector. The survey findings are thus instrumental for policy making, particularly in advancing the Affordable Housing  Program  (AHP)  under  Kenya's  BottomUp Economic Transformation Agenda (BETA) and tracking progress towards housing-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)."},{"p":558,"t":"20.2. Survey Objectives: The primary objective of the 2023/24 Kenya Housing Survey was to provide up-to-date statistics on housing in Kenya to inform policy formulation. The specific objectives of the survey were to:"},{"p":558,"t":"Assess the quality and condition of housing units."},{"p":558,"t":"Evaluate the level of access to infrastructure and services"},{"p":558,"t":"Evaluate housing affordability by providing insights into housing costs and rent."},{"p":558,"t":"Assess the level of awareness and perceptions regarding the Affordable Housing Program, housing regulations, and comfort in dwellings."},{"p":558,"t":"Track progress towards national housing goals (Vision 2030 and (SDG) 1, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 16 and 17 indicators related to housing)."},{"p":558,"t":"Assess the change in land consumption using geospatial tools from 2016 to 2023."},{"p":558,"t":"20.3. Survey Design and Scope: The survey employed a cross-sectional study design to collect data for estimating housing indicators at national, rural, urban and county levels. To achieve this, a hybrid data collection system was incorporated, targeting both households and institutions. The household component of the survey was designed independently from that of the institutions. A sample survey was conducted for the households, while a census was carried out for all identified institutions key in the housing sector. The survey covered all the 47 counties to ensure that the coverage was comprehensive and representative of the entire country. The household component targeted residential housing units in both urban and rural areas while the institutional component targeted housing developers, real estate firms, Water Service Providers, County GovernmentsPhysical Planning Departments, National Environment Management Authority (NEMA) and Land Administration Department. The professional component targeted members from Engineers Board of Kenya (EBK), Kenya Institute of Planners (KIPs) and Board of Registration of Architects and Quantity Surveyors (BORAQS)."},{"p":558,"t":"20.4. Sample  Selection: The  sample  for  the household component of the survey was drawn from the Kenya Household Master Sample Frame (K-HMSF), which was developed based on the 2019 Kenya Population and Housing Census. A total of 1,295 clusters were randomly selected from the sampling  frame  and  25,900  households  within these clusters were selected for interviews. The institutional component of the survey was conducted using multiple lists obtained from various sources, including the State Department for Housing and Urban Development and Water Service Regulatory Board. The Built Environment Professionals (BEPs) list  was sourced from Engineers Board of Kenya (EBK), Kenya Institute of Planners (KIPs) and Board of Registration of Architects and Quantity Surveyors (BORAQS). From this, a census was undertaken for all developers, water service providers, real estate firms and built environment professionals."},{"p":558,"t":"20.5. Data collection and reference period: The data collection for the 2023/24 KHS was undertaken across all the 47 counties from 7th March to 17th May 2024. The reference period for the survey was 2020 to 2024."},{"p":558,"t":"20.6. Response Rates: Out of the 25,900 households sampled for the survey, 23,166 were found to be eligible. A household is considered eligible if it meets one of the following criteria: it is identified and successfully interviewed; it is identified but refuses to respond; it is identified but no competent respondent is available at home during visits; or it is identified, the interview is started and then postponed. Among the  eligible  households,  21,347  completed  the questionnaire, resulting in a national household response rate of 92.1 per cent. The response rate was higher in rural areas at 94.0 per cent compared to 89.9 per cent in urban areas as shown in Table 20.1."},{"p":559,"t":"Table 20.1: Household Response Rate by Residence"},{"section":"The 2023/24 Kenya Housing Survey Findings"},{"p":559,"t":"The 2023/24 Kenya Housing Survey Findings"},{"p":559,"t":"20.7. Housing Tenure-ship: Table 20.2 shows the proportion of homeowners and tenants by residence presented by different categories of housing tenure. Nationally, 61.3 per cent of the households were homeowners, while 33.4 per cent paid rent/lease. Analysis by residence showed that households in rural areas had higher rates of home ownership (85.5%) compared to households in urban areas (22.8%). Conversely, urban areas had a higher proportion of households paying rent/leasing (72.3%) than rural"},{"p":559,"t":"The sample for the household component of the survey was drawn from the Kenya Household Master Sample Frame (K-HMSF), which was developed based on the 2019 Kenya Population and Housing Census."},{"p":559,"t":"areas (8.9%). The proportion of home ownership in rural areas was highest in Isiolo (98.0%), Marsabit (97.5%), and Baringo (96.9%), while in urban areas home ownership was highest in Wajir at 87.0 per cent. The proportion of Squatters was highest in Mandera (14.0%), Lamu (9.0%), and Garissa (6.2%). The proportion of tenants who did not pay rent with the consent of owners was highest in Lamu (19.3%), Mandera (18.9%), and Nyandarua (12.0%)."},{"p":560,"t":"Table 20.2: Distribution of Households by Tenure-ship Status, County and Residence, 2023/24"},{"p":561,"t":"Table 20.2: Distribution of Households by Tenure-ship Status, County and Residence, 2023/24 (Continued)"},{"p":562,"t":"20.8. Dwelling Units by Type: Bungalows were the dominant type of dwelling unit in the country, accounting for 51.8 per cent of all dwelling unit. Its indicators play a pivotal role in informing housing sector planning, financial stability and economic policy formulation. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) identifies the real estate market as an important source of indicators relevant for both financial and non-financial sectors. In particular, the real estate index is used as a deflator in the balance sheet and fixed capital formation in the compilation of national accounts statistics. Further, residential property prices are a key indicator in measuring growth as they reflect the affordability of residen -tial units by households, either through ownership or rental arrangements."},{"p":572,"t":"20.23 .Commercial properties, on the other hand, provide economic agents with space for business operations and are mainly categorized by form of usage which includes office space/proper -ty, retail space/property, industrial property such as, warehousing and storage, factories among others. Whereas the Consumer Price Index (CPI) captures information on monthly rent, the current housing market supply side data was missing, necessitating the carrying out of the 2023/24 Real  Estate  Survey to provide indicators on property p r i c e s a n d rent  as well as demand and supply side indicators."},{"section":"Design and Methodology"},{"p":572,"t":"Design and Methodology"},{"p":572,"t":"20.24. Survey objective: The overall objective of the 2023/24 Real Estate Survey was to collect data on prices for both residential and commercial properties. The specific ob -jectives were to provide; up-to-date market statistics on the real estate sector, information on trends in typologies for housing, information on prices and rent for residential and commercial properties and update the statistical business register"},{"p":572,"t":"(SBR) for the sector."},{"p":572,"t":"20.25. Scope and target population: The 2023/24 Real Estate Survey targeted all establishments engaged in real estate activities drawn from (SBR). The classification was based on the Kenya Standard Indus -trial Classification of all Economic Activities (KeSIC) used to classify economic entities (units) according to activities in which they are engaged in. The real estate activities covered in the survey were real estate activities with own or leased property. This included buying and selling of residential and non-residential buildings; renting and operating of real estate; operating of furnished/serviced apartments, and activities of real estate developers of own property for leasing or sale (construction work done by a separate entity at a fee)."},{"p":572,"t":"20.26. The survey also covered real estate activities on a fee or contract basis such as activities of real estate agents and brokers; intermediation in buying, selling and renting of real estate; and management of real estate. The survey collected information from institutions engaged in all real estate activities including real estate agents, property managers as well as developers. To supplement survey data, secondary data on real estate properties sold was obtained from real estate establishments and agents."},{"p":572,"t":"20.27. Reference period: The 2023/24 Real Estate Survey collected information for real estate properties that were on offer for sale, rent, sold and rented out/leased in 2023. In this regard, information was collected for properties that were advertised for sale/ let before 2023 and were still on offer for sale/let in 2023; properties advertised forsale/let before 2023 and were sold/ let during 2023, properties that were advertised for sale/ let in 2023 and were actually sold/ let in 2023; and properties that were advertised for sale/let in 2023 but were not sold during 2023."},{"section":"Survey Findings"},{"p":572,"t":"Survey Findings"},{"p":572,"t":"20.28. This survey has been carried out every 2-3 years since 2006 with an objective of enhancing financial inclusion measurement and provide indicators to track progress in financial inclusion in Kenya. The 2024 Survey is unique in several"},{"p":581,"t":"PAGE 541"},{"p":582,"t":"ways. First, it provides the first data point for time series analysis of the change in financial inclusion at the county level. The findings at county level highlight the heterogeneity of the financial inclusion landscape. Secondly,  the  questionnaire  was  expanded  to improve the measurement of the four dimensions of financial inclusion: Access, Usage, Quality and Impact. Emerging issues such as sustainable finance (green finance), financial health, Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs), Agriculture, and Persons with Disabilities (PWDs) were also covered. This chapter presents highlights from the 2024 FinAccess Household Survey report which is available on the FinAccess portal on the KNBS website."},{"section":"Survey Objectives"},{"p":582,"t":"Survey Objectives"},{"p":582,"t":"21.2. The main objective of FinAccess Household Surveys is to monitor developments and progress achieved  in  financial  inclusion.  It  also  enables policy makers and industry players to gain a better understanding and dynamics of Kenya's financial inclusion landscape. The specific survey objectives were:"},{"p":582,"t":"Tracking  trends  and  progress  on  financial inclusion."},{"p":582,"t":"Providing information on barriers to financial inclusion."},{"p":582,"t":"Providing information on market conditions and opportunities."},{"p":582,"t":"Providing  data  for  academic  research  on financial inclusion."},{"section":"Survey Methodology"},{"p":582,"t":"Survey Methodology"},{"p":582,"t":"21.3. The 2024 FinAccess Household Survey was a cross-sectional survey that targeted individuals aged 16 years and above residing in conventional households in Kenya. However, the report is based on analysis of data for individuals aged 18 years and above, as national identity cards, which is a key requirement to accessing formal financial services, only issued upon attaining this age. The survey sample was drawn from the Kenya Household Master Sample Frame (K-HMSF), which was developed based on the 2019 Kenya Population and Housing Census. The sample was designed to provide estimates at national as well as rural and urban areas, and across the fortyseven (47) counties. The minimum sample size was computed for each of the survey domains, resulting in a total sample size of 28,275 households and 1,885 Enumeration Areas (EAs). The survey results achieved 84.6 per cent response rate."},{"section":"Key Findings Classification of Financial Services and Products"},{"p":582,"t":"Key Findings Classification of Financial Services and Products"},{"p":582,"t":"21.4. Financial services and products were classified according  to  the  most  frequently  used  formal financial service provider. Respondents who used at least one formal financial service provider were classified  as  formally  included.  Formal  financial service providers include banks, Savings and Credit Cooperatives (SACCOs), mobile phone financial service providers (MFSs) and insurance companies, among other providers. Respondents who did not use any formal provider but used at least one informal service provider (e.g. groups, chamas, shopkeeper credit, etc.) were classified as informally included. Respondents who did not use any formal or informal service providers were classified as financially excluded. The excluded population comprises of those that relied on basic financial mechanisms such as saving in secret/ hiding places or borrowing from a family member or a friend in case of a need."},{"section":"Overall Financial Access"},{"p":582,"t":"Overall Financial Access"},{"p":582,"t":"21.5. Overall access to formal financial services has seen a substantial increase, expanding from the baseline of 26.7 per cent in 2006 to 84.8 per cent in 2024 (Figure 21.1). This growth is on account of financial technology and innovations, especially in mobile money, and physical expansion as formal financial services providers take services closer to the population. This was also supported by a conducive regulatory environment and various Government initiatives toward an inclusive financial system."},{"p":582,"t":"21.6. The survey findings show that financial exclusion has decreased by 1.7 percentage points to 9.9 per cent in 2024, mainly on account of an increase in informal utilization of financial services."},{"p":583,"t":"Figure 21.1: Overall Financial Access"},{"section":"Financial Access by Products"},{"p":583,"t":"Financial Access by Products"},{"p":583,"t":"21.7. Access to various financial products and ser -vices in 2024 and 2021 are presented in Figure 21.2. Access to banking services increased to 52.5 per cent in 2024 from 44.1 per cent in 2021. The 'Used to use' cases increased between 2021 and 2024, with loss of income and cost of financial services being cited as the main reasons. 'Never used' cases cited affordability, relevance and awareness as limitations to access. The main reason for 'Never Used' of mobile money was due to lack of a mobile phone and eligibility due to lack of Identity cards."},{"p":583,"t":"8"},{"p":584,"t":"Figure 21.2: Financial Access by Products (per cent)"},{"section":"Financial Exclusion by Age Group and Residence"},{"p":585,"t":"Financial Exclusion by Age Group and Residence"},{"p":585,"t":"21.8. The survey results indicated that 45.6 per cent of young people aged 18-25 in rural areas were formally excluded. Similarly, 12.4 per cent of older people aged 55 and above in rural areas were finan -cially excluded compared with 1.5 per cent of their counterparts in urban areas as shown in (Figure 21.3). The lack of national Identification Cards (IDs) among young people aged 18-25 largely explains the high exclusion rate."},{"p":585,"t":"Figure 21.3: Financial Exclusion by Age Group and Residence"},{"section":"Financial Access by Sex"},{"p":585,"t":"Financial Access by Sex"},{"p":585,"t":"21.9. Access to formal financial services and products between the female and male population narrowed to 1.6 percentage points in 2024 from 4.2 percentage points in 2021. This was a result of continued low uptake by male and increased access by female to 84.1 per cent in 2024 from 81.7 per cent in 2021 as shown in Figure 21.4."},{"p":585,"t":"Figure 21.4: Formal Inclusion Gender Gap"},{"section":"Formal Financial Access by Residence"},{"p":586,"t":"Formal Financial Access by Residence"},{"p":586,"t":"21.10. Urban population recorded the highest access to formal financial services and products through formal financial providers at 91.3 per cent compared to 80.2 per cent for the rural population as shown in Figure 21.5. The rural-urban gap in access to formal financial services providers continued to narrow on account of continued penetration of formal financial services providers through digital channels and physical channels like branches and agents."},{"p":586,"t":"Figure 21.5: Formal Financial Access by Residence"},{"section":"Financial Access by County"},{"p":586,"t":"Financial Access by County"},{"p":586,"t":"21.11. Kiambu county had the highest formal financial inclusion at 94.0 per cent, while West Pokot County had the lowest formal financial inclusion at 48.5 per cent as shown in Table 21.1.  On the other hand, Turkana County had the highest level of exclusion at 31.5 per cent as shown in Table 21.2."},{"p":587,"t":"Table 21.1: Formal Financial Inclusion in 2024"},{"p":587,"t":"Table 21.2: Formal Financial Exclusion in 2024"},{"section":"Usage of Financial Services and Products by Provider"},{"p":588,"t":"Usage of Financial Services and Products by Provider"},{"p":588,"t":"21.12. The 2024 FinAccess Survey highlights shifting financial preferences in Kenya, with mobile money usage rising to 82.3 per cent in 2024 from 79.4 per cent in 2019. This is driven by expanded services, improved infrastructure, and greater adoption for Government services and e-commerce. Bank usage increased to 52.5 per cent, supported by infrastructure development. SACCOs participation grew to 11.7 per cent, reflecting broader reach, while informal group membership rose to 30.8 per cent, emphasizing the continued importance of grassroots financial systems. Investments in securities increased to 3.1 percent, boosted by new applications (apps) and higher returns, showcasing diverse consumer financial needs. In contrast, insurance uptake saw a slight decline to 22.0 per cent (Figure 21.6)."},{"p":588,"t":"Figure 21.6: Usage of Financial Services and Products"},{"section":"Usage by the Portfolio of Financial Services"},{"p":588,"t":"Usage by the Portfolio of Financial Services"},{"p":588,"t":"21.13. The survey findings indicate that consumers are transitioning from using a single service to using many services. The number of respondents who use two or more types of financial services and products increased to 79.7 per cent in 2024 from 75.3 per cent in 2021 (Figure 21.7). This reflects the increasing complexity of financial needs, which require a range of financial products and services to address them. However, the marginal decline in non-usage to 9.9 per cent suggests that some segments, particularly those without national IDs, still face barriers to accessing financial services."},{"p":589,"t":"Figure 21.7: Usage by the Portfolio of Financial Services"},{"section":"Traditional Banking and Mobile Bank Usage"},{"p":589,"t":"Traditional Banking and Mobile Bank Usage"},{"p":589,"t":"21.14. The overall usage of traditional banking (Banking operations through physical branch networks) increased from 23.8 per cent in 2021 to 29.0 per cent in 2024 as shown in Figure 21.8.  However, the overall usage of mobile banking slightly decreased to 32.6 per cent in 2024 from 34.4 per cent in 2021(Figure 21.9)."},{"p":589,"t":"Figure 21.8: Traditional Banking Usage"},{"p":589,"t":"Figure 21.9: Mobile Bank Usage"},{"section":"The Financial Inclusion Fund (The Hustler Fund)"},{"p":590,"t":"The Financial Inclusion Fund (The Hustler Fund)"},{"p":590,"t":"21.15. The usage of Hustler Fund loan services for the urban population was 35.4 per cent while the rural population usage was 24.2 per cent. Usage of the fund among the male population was 31.8 per cent while female population usage was at 26.1 per cent. Usage of Hustler Fund by wealth quintiles indicates that 35.8 per cent of those in the highest wealth quintile use Hustler Fund, while 18.7 per cent of those in the lowest wealth quintile use Hustler Fund (Figure 21.10)."},{"p":590,"t":"Figure 21.10: Financial Inclusion Fund (The Hustler Fund) Usage by Demographics (per cent)"},{"section":"Insurance, Pensions and Investments Usage"},{"p":590,"t":"Insurance, Pensions and Investments Usage"},{"p":590,"t":"21.16.
